summary:
Smoke inhalation causes death mainly via carbon monoid oxide-induced hypoxemia, impairing oxygen transport by forming carboxyhemoglobin that reduces available oxygens. This results in inadequate tissue perfusion leading to fatal systemic ischemia despite normal arterial blood gases.

The summary above correctly identifies the primary pathophysiological pathway—the impairment of oxygen transport from lungs to tissues—and emphasizes its role in causing lethal outcomes during fire-related deaths.
